title: Stripe Native Reports vs Third-Party Revenue Trackers
meta_description: A former engineer breaks down what Stripe's built-in reports actually show, where they lie to you, and when a third-party MRR tool earns its keep.
h1: Stripe Native Reports vs Third-Party Revenue Trackers: What's Actually Different
body_markdown:
The first time I trusted Stripe's native MRR number, I was off by $2,140. Not a rounding error. A real, reconciled gap between what Stripe's Sigma query told me and what my bookkeeper pulled from the subscription ledger a week later. I spent two evenings digging through Stripe's docs before I figured out why — and honestly, I'm still a little annoyed about it.

So let's talk about the real difference between Stripe's native reports and third-party revenue trackers. Not the marketing-page difference. The actual, you-shipped-this-feature-and-now-you-maintain-it difference. I built an internal MRR dashboard for a company doing about $180k MRR on Stripe between 2021 and 2023, and I've since rebuilt variations of it three more times. This is what I learned.

## What Stripe actually gives you out of the box

Stripe's native reporting is better than people give it credit for. The Dashboard → Billing → Overview page shows you MRR, active subscribers, churned revenue, and a trailing chart. For a founder doing $3k MRR with 40 customers on two plans, it's probably enough. I mean it. Don't overbuild.

But the moment your billing gets even slightly weird — and it will — the native reports start quietly lying to you. Not maliciously. Stripe is making defensible product decisions about how to aggregate data. Those decisions just don't always match how you, the founder, actually think about your revenue.

Here's where the native reports get uncomfortable:

- **Annual plans.** Stripe's MRR calc divides annual subscriptions by 12. Fine. But if you offer a discount on annual (and you should — Kyle Poyar at OpenView wrote a pretty good piece in 2022 about annual plan discounts landing around 15-20%), the MRR number includes the discount amortized across 12 months, while your cash in the bank is one lump payment today. The chart and the bank account disagree.
- **Trials and scheduled subscriptions.** Stripe counts MRR from the moment a subscription becomes active. Since their 2023 subscription schedules update, you can queue up future price changes, but the dashboard's MRR number doesn't always reflect committed-but-not-started revenue the way you'd expect.
- **Failed payments.** When a card fails, Stripe's Smart Retries kick in. During the retry window (typically 3 weeks), is that customer churned? Stripe says no. Your gut probably says partially. Your investors, if you have any, definitely say yes.
- **Refunds mid-cycle.** If you refund a $99/mo customer on day 15, does MRR drop by $99 that month or next? Stripe has an answer. It may not match yours.

I got the annual-plan thing wrong for six months. I was pitching investors off a number that was accurate-but-confusing, and one of them caught it in a second meeting. That was fun.

## Sigma is powerful and also kind of a trap

Okay, so the built-in dashboard has limits. The obvious Stripe-native answer is Sigma, their SQL interface over your Stripe data. It costs $10/mo for basic and scales with event volume. I used it heavily. It's genuinely good.

And it's also where a lot of founders burn a weekend they shouldn't have.

Here's the thing about Sigma: the schema is more complicated than it looks. The `subscriptions` table doesn't just have active subscriptions — it has every subscription state, and you need to filter on `status IN ('active', 'trialing', 'past_due')` depending on how you count. The `invoices` table has line items that don't map cleanly to subscriptions when proration is involved. Coupons apply at the invoice level, not the subscription level. Tax is its own adventure.

I wrote what I thought was a clean MRR query. It disagreed with the dashboard by about 3%. I wrote a second query to figure out why. That one disagreed with both. After about the fourth iteration I realized I was basically rebuilding ChartMogul from scratch, poorly, on a Tuesday night.

This is the trap. Sigma makes you feel like you're close to the answer. You're not. You're close to a version of the answer that will be wrong in a way you won't notice until it matters.

## What third-party trackers actually do differently

The honest version: most third-party revenue trackers are wrappers around Stripe's API with opinionated MRR logic baked in. That's not a dig. The opinions are the product.

Baremetrics, ChartMogul, ProfitWell (now Paddle Retention, after the 2022 acquisition) — they've each spent years arguing with customers about edge cases and have converged on roughly similar MRR definitions. ChartMogul publishes theirs. Baremetrics does too. The definitions differ in small ways (how they treat one-time charges, how they handle reactivations within 30 days, whether annual discounts are amortized gross or net) but they're internally consistent, and that's what matters.

Internal consistency is the whole game.

If your MRR number uses the same definition every day, trends mean something. If you rewrote your Sigma query last Thursday because you noticed a bug, your June-vs-July comparison is now suspect. I've done this to myself. More than once.

The other thing third-party tools do that's genuinely hard to replicate: cohort retention and revenue churn cuts. Net revenue retention by signup month is the single most useful SaaS metric I know of, and writing the SQL for it against raw Stripe data is a real project. Patrick Campbell used to say something like "NRR is the only metric that matters at scale" — he said it often enough that I'll count it as a quote.

> Your MRR chart tells you what happened. Your cohort retention tells you whether to keep going.

## Where each approach actually wins

If you're doing under about $5k MRR with simple monthly pricing, Stripe's native dashboard is fine. Use it. Don't pay $129/mo for Baremetrics' Startup plan when your entire revenue number fits on a sticky note. I see founders on Indie Hackers do this constantly and it's just cost theater.

If you're between $5k and $50k MRR with any of the following — annual plans, multiple currencies, usage-based pricing, coupons, or a trial flow more complex than "14 days then charge" — the native dashboard will mislead you, and rolling your own Sigma queries will eat more weekends than it's worth. This is the band where a third-party tracker pays for itself, roughly, by saving you from making one bad decision based on a wrong number.

If you're past $50k MRR, you probably need both. Third-party for the daily number and the board deck. Sigma (or a proper warehouse sync via Fivetran or Stripe's own data pipeline) for the weird questions nobody else can answer. At that scale, hiring a half-time analyst starts making sense too.

One thing I want to be direct about: I think ProfitWell's free tier, post-Paddle-acquisition, is a worse product than it was in 2021. The UI changes have been rough and the metrics feel less trustworthy to me than they did. I know people who disagree. But if you're picking a free option today, ChartMogul's launch plan (free up to $10k MRR as of their 2023 pricing change) is where I'd start.

## The thing nobody tells you about switching later

Here's a small, annoying truth. Whichever tool you pick, your historical MRR number will change when you switch. Not because anyone's lying — because the definitions differ. I've watched a founder's June 2022 MRR shift by $400 between two tools. Same raw Stripe data. Different opinions about what counts.

So pick a definition. Write it down somewhere. If you switch tools, note the switchover date in your dashboard and stop pretending the pre-switch and post-switch numbers are the same series. They aren't.

This is also, incidentally, the argument for a lightweight tracker that just reads your Stripe data and shows you a clean MRR number without trying to be a full analytics suite. Something like [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) is built for the $1k-$50k MRR founder who doesn't want to log into Stripe six times a day and doesn't need cohort analysis yet — just a trustworthy number and a chart they can glance at. That's a real gap between the native dashboard and the full Baremetrics-tier tools, and it's where I spend most of my attention these days.

The real question isn't native vs third-party. It's whether you know, today, what definition your MRR number uses. If you can't answer that in one sentence, the tool you're using is wrong for you — whichever one it is.
target_keyword: stripe native reports vs third-party revenue trackers
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background with muted teal and amber accents. Abstract concept: two overlapping translucent data charts misaligned by a small visible gap, rendered as geometric line art with subtle grid texture. One chart is rigid and boxy, the other softer and curved, suggesting two different interpretations of the same underlying data. Minimal composition, lots of negative space, faint scatter of data points like distant stars. Premium tech magazine aesthetic, flat vector style with subtle gradient depth, no text, no people, no logos.
